dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a reported chest and abdominal pain at a residence on Belgian Street.
Hello, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911 this morning?
Chest pain and stomach discomfort. It feels like someone is squeezing my chest. It's been like this for about three days.
What is your name please?
Michael.
Can you tell me the name of the city we are in?
Richmond.
Can you tell me why we are here?
Chest pain and something strange in my stomach.
Were you the one who placed the 911 call today?
Yes, it got bad this morning, so I called.
Let's do a few quick assessments. Are you comfortable with that?
Yes, go ahead.
Airway is patent and maintainable, no signs of obstruction or difficulty breathing.
Let me check your breathing pattern.
Rate is 16 per minute, depth is normal, and work of breathing is unlabored.
Bilateral breath sounds are equal. No adventitious sounds noted.
I'm checking your pulse now.
Can we take your blood pressure now?
Blood pressure is 121 over 87.
Can you tell me about your daily routine?
I usually deliver goods, but I've been home resting recently.
So your lifestyle hasn't changed much recently?
No, it's been completely normal.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how bad is your chest pain now?
It's an 8 out of 10.
Per protocol, we are doing a 4-lead ECG first to establish a baseline.
We'll then do a full 12-lead ECG for more detail on your heart activity.
Oxygen saturation is at 100% on room air.
Current EKG shows sinus tachycardia with no signs of elevation or depression.
I'm placing the 12-lead ECG electrodes now. This is standard for chest pain cases.
12-lead is complete. We are seeing abnormal Q-waves in aVF and small R in V3.
There's also an inferior ischemic T noted and slight ST-T abnormalities in V1.
As per protocol, I will give you 324 mg of aspirin. Is that okay?
Yes, go ahead.
What medications have you been taking?
I take Cymbalta and Zantac every day.
Any allergies to medicine or food?
No known allergies.
Any medical conditions we should know about?
Yes, I have insulin-dependent diabetes and high blood pressure.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
I had coffee about an hour ago.
Do you have any idea how the pain started?
No, it just developed gradually.
Your pulse is now 112 and regular.
Blood pressure is 132 over 84. It's stable.
Your oxygen is at 97% now.
EKG remains sinus tachycardia with no new changes.
Your glucose is undetectable, which is common with insulin-dependent diabetes.
Following protocol, we'll transport you to Richmond Community Hospital for further evaluation. Are you ready?
I'll assist you to the ambulance now. Just trying to ensure a smooth transfer.
All secured in the seat. Vital signs remain stable during the transfer.
You are now safe and secure during transport to the hospital.
We have a 38-year-old male with reported chest and stomach pain. GCS is 15, 12-lead ECG shows suspected inferoapical infarct.
Current vitals: pulse 112, BP 132/84, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 97, and glucose undetectable.
324 mg of aspirin has been given per protocol with no adverse reactions.
EKG has been re-checked. Sinus tachycardia with abnormal Q-wave in aVF, small R in V3, and minimal ST-T changes in V1.
Patient is responsive and stable. No complications during loading.
Baseline vitals and responses are consistent. Medical team is aware of all findings.
Let me know if you need further details or support during transfer.
I've got the report. Thanks for the thorough documentation and handling of the case.
Appreciate the pickup. You have a very clear situation and patient summary.
Let me re-check: pulse is still 112, respiratory rate stays 16, blood pressure 132/84, SpO2 at 97%.
No additional medications or procedures needed at this time.
38-year-old with insulin-dependent diabetes and hypertension. Medications are Cymbalta and Zantac.
EKG finds concern with abnormal Q-wave in aVF, small R in V3, and ST-T changes in V1. Minimal LVH in V1.
Aspirin administered at 07:40 with no change noted per protocol.
No changes since initial assessment. Transport remaining stable.
Approximately five minutes to ER. All prepared for transfer.
No updates to document. Transport stays within expected protocol.
Stretcher boarding assistance will be available when we arrive.
Receiving physician is en route. We have complete control and documentation ready.
I'll support the transfer if needed. Everything seems ready from this end.
Thank you for the excellent work and detailed field report.
Patient positioned and secured-no movement during transport.
Approaching the hospital. Preparing for transfer to ER now.
All findings documented as per 2-1 protocol. Handing over for hospital care.
Double-checked all field impression and documentation items-complete and ready.
On route back in 20 minutes if no holdups.
As per final check, EKG still reflects ST segment and T wave changes in lead V1 suggesting mild or early changes.
When did the chest pain begin exactly?
It started three days ago, gradually. This is the worst I've felt.
Given your history of insulin-dependent diabetes and hypertension, these can influence cardiac conditions. Understood?
Yes, I know. It's still unusual for my chest to feel like this.
Do you have any nausea or sweating with the chest discomfort?
No, it's just the squeezing.
Your blood sugar level remains undetectable. Do you carry insulin with you?
Yes, it was prescribed to me today. I don't use it without a blood sugar meter.
No asymmetry in chest movements. Respiration effort has not increased.
You mentioned coffee an hour ago. Have you had any food in the last 24 hours?
No food yet. Morning routine is coffee first.
Considering the 12-lead results, Richmond Community Hospital has EKG interpretation and prompt cardiology coverage. Good choice.
ST changes in V1 are not clear determinants of myocardial infarction but suggest subtle ischaemia. Re-check in the ER.
You take Zantac regularly-is your stomach pain related or separate from the chest issue?
Not sure. It feels more connected to the chest-like it started there and spread.
Interviewing shows no signs of torso or limb trauma. No impact injuries noted during assessment or transport.
Blood pressure remains between 120/80 and 135/86-no unusual spikes for tachycardia.
You'll be taken to a hospital with prompt interventional cardiology if infarct is confirmed.
Be advised-paramedic performed all monitoring and protocol check steps. Final EKG unchanged.
No interventions occurred post-aspirin. Management followed EKG monitoring and education on presenting to the ER.
